judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently and correctly identify all three root causes with nearly identical depth and accuracy; Report 2 adds a few extra touches (e.g., noting the weak test coverage more explicitly, a connection-leak aside) but both fully satisfy all expectations.

report_1.evidence: cedar_owner is the role that scripts/migrate.sh uses to run all DDL in 0001_init.sql, making it the owner of the clients/appointments tables... By standard, documented PostgreSQL RLS semantics, row-security policies are never evaluated for the owning role — and never for superusers — unless the table has ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, which 0001_init.sql never sets. So every GET /clients request... executes against a role for which the tenant_isolation policy is a complete no-op

report_2.evidence: cedar_owner is not an ordinary role — it's the POSTGRES_USER from docker-compose.yml, which the official postgres Docker image bootstraps as the cluster's superuser... it's also the role that owns every table because scripts/migrate.sh runs all migrations as cedar_owner. In PostgreSQL, row-level security is unconditionally bypassed for superusers, and by default also bypassed for the owner of a table (the migration never applies FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY...)

report_1.evidence: Treat "migration/owner" credentials and "application runtime" credentials as categorically separate, never interchangeable: cedar_owner should be used only by scripts/migrate.sh/CI migration jobs, never embeddable in the app's runtime config... change DATABASE_URL in cedarline/.env (and the checked-in cedarline/.env.example...) from cedar_owner to cedar_app

report_2.evidence: Enforce the two-role split as a hard boundary, not a convention. cedar_owner should be used only by scripts/migrate.sh (or a CI/CD migration step) and should never appear in any config the running application reads... Immediate fix: change DATABASE_URL in cedarline/.env from cedar_owner to cedar_app credentials

report_1.evidence: Creating cedar_app is, by design, a separate manual step... the script itself says "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup." That framing ("first setup") is the trap: it's actually a one-time-per-cluster step, and reset-local-db.sh silently creates a new cluster every time it runs, without re-running it.

report_2.evidence: cedar_app is created by exactly one script, cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql, which is explicitly commented "One-time bootstrap ... Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup."... scripts/reset-local-db.sh runs docker compose down -v (this deletes the named volume cedar_pgdata...) → docker compose up -d db (a brand-new cluster is initialized; only cedar_owner/database cedar are recreated...) → ./scripts/migrate.sh (recreates schema/tables/RLS policies/seed rows, but no roles beyond what Postgres itself bootstraps).
